food_subgroup: Cooked beef dishes — spicy salad preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai spicy beef salad slice; closest parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_03302757) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Beef meat dishes) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for Thai-style spicy beef salad.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai beef salad preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai spicy beef salad slice is approximately 150 g, comprising thin slices of cooked beef tossed in a tangy lime-fish sauce dressing with chilli, shallots, and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, round, top round, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 13322); adapted with Thai salad dressing component adjustments based on USDA FoodData Central entries for lime juice, fish sauce, and chilli
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai spicy beef salad slice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. USDA SR Legacy cooked lean beef (top round, braised) used as the primary protein-fat-energy anchor. Dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, shallots, herbs) contribute minimal macros but meaningful sodium. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite of approximately 70% cooked lean beef and 30% dressing/vegetable components, consistent with the dish description. Sodium elevated to reflect fish sauce contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on cooked lean beef (GI ~0) and low-GI salad dressing components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai spicy beef salad slice. Cooked lean beef has a GI of effectively 0 (negligible carbohydrate). The dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, shallots) contribute a small carbohydrate load with low GI. A composite GI of 10 is assigned as a conservative low estimate, consistent with the International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., 2021) methodology for mixed meat-and-vegetable dishes with minimal starch. GL per 150 g serving = (10 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.57, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce in dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 150 g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce; requesting reduced fish sauce or a lighter dressing can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 10, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugars or high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean protein with relatively low saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the sodium content from fish sauce warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Spicy Beef Salad Slice (known in Thai as เนื้อสไลซ์ยำ or Yam Nuea) is a Thai dish consisting of thin slices of cooked beef — typically grilled or briefly braised — tossed in a tangy, spicy salad dressing made from lime juice, fish sauce, fresh chilli, shallots, lemongrass, garlic, and fresh herbs such as mint and coriander. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly warm, offering a bright, sour, spicy, and savoury flavour profile characteristic of Thai yam (salad) preparations. The texture contrasts tender, thinly sliced beef with the crunch of shallots and the freshness of herbs. Nutritionally, this dish is high in protein (approximately 21.5 g per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (3.8 g per 100 g), making it a protein-dense, low-glycaemic option. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce in the dressing (approximately 520 mg per 100 g). The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycaemic load, and is a reasonable protein source for those following a lower-carbohydrate dietary pattern. Sodium moderation is advised for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is available via food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai spicy beef slice?
Is Yam Nuea good for diabetics?

beef slices: เนื้อสไลซ์

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai spicy beef salad slice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lean beef with dressing component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary protein-fat-energy anchor (cooked lean beef) is well-supported by USDA SR Legacy, and the dressing components contribute minimally to macros.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food entry. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are adapted estimates from USDA SR Legacy cooked lean beef (top round, braised) and are not independently verified for this specific Thai restaurant preparation. Sodium is elevated above the base beef value to reflect fish sauce contribution in the dressing; this is an estimate and actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. The food_name has been normalised from the menu label 'S2 Beef Slice' to the canonical English descriptor 'Thai Spicy Beef Salad Slice'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce sourcing and preparation cannot be confirmed without restaurant verification. GI set to 10 as an analogous low estimate; glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (10 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.57, rounded to 0.6 for a 150 g serving.
